reference_notes: No Python reference registered for this method.
R `spls` 2.3.2 (Chun & Keles). Predicts via the regression coefficient matrix from sparse-thresholded SIMPLS.
Nikzad-Langerodi, R., Zellinger, W., Saminger-Platz, S., Moser, B. A. (2018). Domain-invariant partial least squares regression. Analytical Chemistry 90(11), 6693-6701.
Moving-window refit using sklearn PLSRegression (NIPALS).
Moving-window refit using R `pls::plsr` (SIMPLS by default).

Indahl, U. G. (2005). A twist to partial least squares regression. Journal of Chemometrics 19(1), 32-44. (Powered PLS — col-std^gamma rescaling. R `pls::cppls` shares the name but implements Liland 2009 Canonical Powered PLS, a different algorithm.)

Weighted PLS computed via sklearn PLSRegression on the sqrt(w)-prescaled centered (X, Y). sklearn is the external PLS engine; the row-scaling is a standard preconditioning step that is mathematically equivalent to weighted PLS.
sklearn PLSRegression on the sqrt(w)-prescaled centered data is mathematically equivalent to weighted PLS. sklearn vs pls4all use NIPALS vs SIMPLS so tolerance is widened to ~1e-1.

description: Robust PLS (Huber IRLS over weighted SIMPLS)
reference_notes: Cummins, D. J. & Andrews, C. W. (1995). Iteratively reweighted partial least squares: a performance analysis by Monte Carlo simulation. Journal of Chemometrics 9(6), 489-507. (No widely installable R / Python port of this Huber-IRLS + SIMPLS variant.)
Ridge-augmented PLS via sklearn PLSRegression on the (X aug, Y aug) matrices — standard data-augmentation trick to fold an L2 penalty into a least-squares-style algorithm.

sklearn PLSRegression on the (X augmented with sqrt(λ)·I, Y augmented with zeros) is the standard data-augmentation trick for L2-penalized PLS. NIPALS vs SIMPLS difference explains the widened tolerance.

Stone, M. & Brooks, R. J. (1990). Continuum regression: cross-validated sequentially constructed prediction embracing ordinary least squares, partial least squares and principal components regression. JRSS-B 52(2), 237-269.

Bro, R. (1996). Multiway calibration. Multilinear PLS. Journal of Chemometrics 10(1), 47-61. (R `NPLStoolbox` exists on CRAN but has heavy transitive deps that don't build in this env; MATLAB `nplstoolbox` is the canonical reference. Python `tensorly` ships PARAFAC but not N-PLS regression.)

Rosipal, R. & Trejo, L. J. (2001). Kernel partial least squares regression in reproducing kernel Hilbert space. JMLR 2, 97-123. (R `pls` implements the linear kernel-algorithm only; `kernlab` ships KPCA but not KPLS; sklearn has no kernel PLS.)
No Python reference registered for this method.
R `OmicsPLS::o2m` (Bouhaddani 2018) implements a joint iterative SVD O2PLS variant — differs from pls4all's peel-then-PLS algorithm. Tolerance widened to admit the expected ~45% RMS divergence.

Eastment, H. T. & Krzanowski, W. J. (1982). Cross-validatory choice of the number of components from a principal component analysis. Technometrics 24(1), 73-77. (R `pls::plsr(validation='LOO')` returns true LOO-PRESS, a different quantity from this leverage-inflated approximation.)
No Python reference registered for this method.

R `mdatools::pls` with `predict()$xdecomp$T2 / $Q`. DModX is derived locally from $Q + DOF. mdatools uses different SIMPLS deflation / normalization conventions than pls4all, so cross-implementation parity is qualitative.
No Python reference registered for this method.

R `mdatools::pls` with `predict()$xdecomp$T2 / $Q`. DModX is derived locally from $Q + DOF. mdatools uses different SIMPLS deflation / normalization conventions than pls4all, so cross-implementation parity is qualitative.

Næs, T., Tomic, O., Mevik, B.-H., Martens, H. (2011). Path modelling by sequential PLS regression. Journal of Chemometrics 25(1), 28-40. (R `multiblock` ships sopls but its transitive deps (`HDANOVA`) fail to install in this env.)

Löfstedt, T. & Trygg, J. (2011). OnPLS — a novel multiblock method for the modelling of predictive and orthogonal variation. Journal of Chemometrics 25(8), 441-455. (R `multiblock::onpls` has the same HDANOVA dep cascade.)

Liland, K. H., Næs, T. & Indahl, U. G. (2016). ROSA — a fast extension of partial least squares regression for multiblock data analysis. Journal of Chemometrics 30(11), 651-662. (R `multiblock` ships rosa with the same dep cascade.)

Breiman, L. (1996). Bagging predictors. Machine Learning 24(2), 123-140. (sklearn ships `BaggingRegressor(PLSRegression())` but RNG / bootstrap-index conventions differ between sklearn and pls4all — numerical parity is impossible without sharing the exact RNG.)

Friedman, J. H. (2001). Greedy function approximation: a gradient boosting machine. Annals of Statistics 29(5), 1189-1232. (sklearn `GradientBoostingRegressor` uses decision-tree weak learners, not PLS; no widely-installable PLS-boosting port exists.)

Ho, T. K. (1998). The random subspace method for constructing decision forests. IEEE TPAMI 20(8), 832-844. (Random-feature-subsample bagging with PLS as the weak learner; same RNG-divergence issue as bagging.)

description: PLS-GLM (§5) — softmax/Poisson IRLS on PLS scores
reference_notes: Bastien, P., Vinzi, V. E. & Tenenhaus, M. (2005). PLS generalised linear regression. Comput. Stat. Data Anal. 48(1), 17-46. (R `plsRglm` ships the Bastien IRLS variant but has heavy transitive deps (car, bipartite) that fail to build in this env. pls4all's implementation is a simplified PLS-then-link variant; algorithmic agreement with plsRglm would be partial at best.)

description: PLS-QDA (§5) — quadratic discriminant on PLS scores
reference_notes: Barker, M. & Rayens, W. (2003). Partial least squares for discrimination. Journal of Chemometrics 17(3), 166-173. (PLS-DA / QDA on PLS scores; sklearn ships `QuadraticDiscriminantAnalysis` but not a PLS+QDA pipeline; the parity would be on the specific scaling of class covariances, where implementations diverge.)

description: PLS-Cox (§5) — Cox PH on PLS scores
reference_notes: Bastien, P. (2008). Deviance residuals based PLS regression for censored data in high dimensional setting. Chemom. Intell. Lab. Syst. 91(1), 78-86. (R `plsRcox` ships this but failed to install in this env via plsRglm/car deps; Python `lifelines` provides Cox PH but not a PLS-reduced variant.)

Wang, Y., Veltkamp, D. J. & Kowalski, B. R. (1991). Multivariate instrument standardization. Analytical Chemistry 63(23), 2750-2756. (Piecewise Direct Standardization; no widely installable R / Python port.)

Wang, Y., Veltkamp, D. J. & Kowalski, B. R. (1991). Multivariate instrument standardization. Analytical Chemistry 63(23), 2750-2756. (Direct Standardization; no widely installable R / Python port — prospectr ships SNV/MSC/Shenk-West but not DS.)

Sjöblom, J., Svensson, O., Josefson, M., Kullberg, H. & Wold, S. (1998). An evaluation of orthogonal signal correction applied to calibration transfer of near infrared spectra. Chemom. Intell. Lab. Syst. 44(1-2), 229-244. (Inverse-regression PLS variant; no widely installable port.)

Nelson, P. R. C., Taylor, P. A. & MacGregor, J. F. (1996). Missing data methods in PCA and PLS: score calculations with incomplete observations. Chemom. Intell. Lab. Syst. 35(1), 45-65. (Missing-aware NIPALS; pls package handles NA via row deletion, not iterative imputation.)
No Python reference registered for this method.

R `spls::splsda` (Chun & Keles). Predictions returned as hard class labels by the package; we one-hot encode them to match pls4all's soft-assignment prediction shape, so the parity check is on the classification *boundary* rather than continuous score values.

Liland, K. H. & Indahl, U. G. (2009). Powered partial least squares discriminant analysis. Journal of Chemometrics 23(1), 7-18. (Group-sparse PLS variants have no widely installable R / Python port; R `sgpls` is for generalized PLS, not group-sparse.)

Yengo, L., Jacques, J., Biernacki, C. & Canouil, M. (2016). Variable clustering in high-dimensional linear regression: The R package `clere`. R Journal 8(1), 92-106. (Fused-sparse PLS variants have no widely installable R / Python port.)
No Python reference registered for this method.
